Un rovescio a una mano, raro nel tennis di oggi, e un gioco elegante e variato: @Lore_Musetti, 24 anni, ha aperto il 2026 conquistando il quinto posto del ranking ATP, prima che un infortunio alla gamba sinistra lo costringesse a rinunciare al Roland Garros. Global ambassador di #BottegaVeneta, il tennista si racconta a @LOfficielItalia, ritratto dagli scatti di @RaffGrosso e con lo styling di @Fernando.Echeverria_, vestendo un total look della collezione #Fall2026, firmato dalla direttrice creativa @Louise_Trotter_. Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!
The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.
From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.
Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”
Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

posts.caption:
Quando l’archivio diventa linguaggio. @SacaiOfficial e @apc_paris si ritrovano per la terza volta, trasformando le quilt della memoria in un’estetica contemporanea. Jessica Ogden, textile artist che da anni dialoga con #APC, ha tradotto i disegni artigianali degli archivi in stampe che abitano sia la collezione donna che uomo. Patchwork tradizionali in nuove palettes, costruzioni moderne che conservano l’anima handcrafted di ogni filo. Disponibile da oggi in boutique, online e presso i retailer selezionati. Perché l’artigianalità non è nostalgia, è linguaggio.
Photo: Courtesy Sacai
#SacaiOfficial #TextileHeritage #APCParis #Craftsmanship

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.alt_text:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum). 

Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood. 

Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.

At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
His work has brought him to countless destinations across the globe, but @TravisScott still has one major stop left on his bucket list. “I hit every continent on the last tour except Antarctica,” he says. “It’s kind of crazy.”
For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.”
Read more about Scott’s adventures at the link in our bio.

posts.alt_text:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!

The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.

From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.

Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”

Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

Global Chief Content Officer @giampietrobaudo

posts.caption:
The 2026 July/August issue is here!
At the center of this year’s Summer Entertainment Issue is @TravisScott, who opens up about working on the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia,’ the travels and experiences fueling his next chapter, and making his acting debut as part of a star-studded ensemble in Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey.’
As much as we love a blockbuster with a stacked A-list cast, championing fresh talent is always top of mind. Inside the issue, we spotlight eight rising stars whose work this summer demands your attention, from ‘Outer Banks’ star @MadisonBaileyBabe to Avantika of ‘Not Suitable for Work’ to @sashacalle of ‘Sugar.’
It’s also a blockbuster time for literary escapes, as the rise of #BookTok turns reading into an unexpected status symbol that’s shaping summer travel. Looking ahead to the Fall collections, we explore the age-old question of whether sex still sells, given the state of undress on the runway, red carpet, and social feeds in 2026. Stay tuned for the issue hitting newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
When @BellaMaclean first stepped onto the ‘Rivals’ set, she knew she was part of something special—but it wasn’t until the first table read that it truly sank in that she would be bringing the world of Jilly Cooper’s iconic ‘Rutshire Chronicles’ to life.  “I remember being like, ‘Oh my God. We’re really making this, and this is actually really happening, and this isn’t a dream,’” she tells L’OFFICIEL.
As Taggie, Maclean inhabits a young woman finding her voice, ambition, and independence. “So much of the time, you want to portray gritty, complicated characters. They’re really fun to play, but I like that Taggie is morally strong,” she says. “She strives for goodness, and it really makes you reflect on how you act in your own life,”
The character certainly left an impact on the actor, who will reprise her role when the second half of Season 2 drops in November. “As the series goes on, she becomes a lot more ballsy, and I felt myself becoming more strong-willed too,” says Maclean. “I took a little piece of her with me.” Read more of our conversation with Bella Maclean at the link in bio.
Editor-in-Chief of @lofficielusa @caroline_grosso⁠

posts.caption:
When ‘Heartstopper’ began filming, Corinna Brown (@itzz_cori) had no idea the tender coming-of-age story would become a global phenomenon. Four years later, her portrayal of Tara has become a defining part of the show’s legacy, resonating with fans who see themselves reflected in the character’s kindness, confidence, and unwavering support for others.
As the ‘Heartstopper’ universe prepares to close with the feature-length finale ‘Heartstopper Forever’ on July 17, Brown is looking ahead to her own next chapter—one centered on championing stories that reflect a range of lived experiences. “I want to show what Black people, women, and queer people go through, because growing up, I didn’t see many stories like that,” she says.
For Brown, the power of storytelling lies in connection. “It shows people they’re not alone, and that there are like-minded people around them,” says the actor, who once met a group of girls who became friends because of ‘Heartstopper.’ “That’s how they found their community,” she says. “I just think that’s so wonderful.” Read more about what’s next for Brown at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“I love playing characters that are so far removed from my own life,” says @miku.martineau.
From a rebellious Yakuza princess in ‘Kate’ to a calculating force in Netflix’s ‘Bet,’ the 21-year-old actor continues to prove her range. Next, she’ll take the lead as an ambitious young coxswain in ‘Crew Girl,’ Netflix’s upcoming YA drama about identity, rivalry, and romance as her character joins an elite all-boys rowing team.
“I think it’s such a universal adolescent experience to not know how to navigate life when it comes at you all at once,” Martineau tells L’OFFICIEL, adding that things for the characters quickly become “messy in the best way.”
Offscreen, Martineau approaches life with the same curiosity. “I really just have fun in the moment,” she says. But she’s also a believer in showing up and putting in the work. “It’s not cringe to try. It’s actually quite beautiful.” Read more of our conversation with Martineau at the link in bio.
Editor-in-Chief of @lofficielusa @caroline_grosso⁠

For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.” 

Read more about Scott’s adventures at the link in our bio. 

Editor-in-Chief of @lofficielusa @caroline_grosso⁠
Photography @gregswalesart

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
For just two weeks each year, the world’s biggest luxury brands compete for a place at Wimbledon and they’re willing to spend millions to do it.
The Championships attract one of the world’s wealthiest and most influential audiences, making it one of the most valuable platforms in luxury marketing. From Rolex timing every match and Ralph Lauren dressing tournament officials to Range Rover transporting players and VIPs, these partnerships offer far more than visibility. They reinforce decades of heritage, craftsmanship, prestige, and global brand recognition.
For luxury houses, Wimbledon isn’t just a tennis tournament. It’s one of the most exclusive stages in the world, where aligning with tradition and excellence is worth every dollar.
#Luxurious #Wimbledon

posts.caption:
For some of the world’s most exclusive restaurants, getting a reservation can be harder than paying the bill.
With months-long waitlists, invitation-only dining rooms, and booking systems that sell out in seconds, these culinary destinations have become symbols of status as much as gastronomy. Whether it’s Tokyo’s legendary sushi counters, New York’s iconic institutions, or intimate Michelin-starred restaurants with just a handful of seats, securing a table often requires patience, persistence, and a little luck.
Here are the hardest restaurant reservations in the world to get.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Paris Fashion Week remains the pinnacle of global fashion, where the world’s most influential maisons unveil the collections that shape the future of luxury.
From unforgettable runway spectacles and historic creative debuts to breathtaking haute couture craftsmanship, the French capital continues to set the benchmark for innovation, artistry, and style. Every season, designers, celebrities, editors, and collectors gather in Paris to witness the moments that define fashion history.
Here are some fascinating facts about Paris Fashion Week that every fashion enthusiast should know.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
McLaren has unveiled the Project: Endurance, giving a select group of owners the opportunity to own a machine developed alongside the brand’s 2027 Le Mans Hypercar.
Created in partnership with McLaren Racing and McLaren Special Operations (MSO), the track-only car is based on the same LMDh platform as McLaren’s upcoming FIA World Endurance Championship challenger. Owners will receive far more than a car—they’ll gain access to a two-year factory-backed driver development program, private track events at world-class circuits, engineering support, race coaching, simulator sessions, and behind-the-scenes access to McLaren’s motorsport expertise.
With only a handful of examples available, Project: Endurance represents one of the most exclusive experiences McLaren has ever offered, bringing customers closer than ever to the world of top-tier endurance racing.
@mclarenauto #McLaren #Luxurious

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.
For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”
Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.
“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.
“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.
Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For years, perfume sprays have dominated our vanities, but solid perfumes are quietly making a strong case for becoming the everyday fragrance essential. No wonder then, that they’re the go-to for brides, being compact, perfect for quick touch-ups and layering and making the wedding-day fragrance last all day.
Unlike alcohol-based sprays that evaporate quickly and can leave some skin types feeling dry, solid perfumes are typically crafted with waxes and nourishing carrier oils like jojoba, sweet almond, or coconut oil. The result? A fragrance that melts into your pulse points, stays close to the skin, and unfolds gradually throughout the day instead of disappearing in a cloud within hours.
Whether you’re a fragrance collector or simply looking for a low-maintenance everyday scent, solid perfumes deserve to be in your pocket.
By Palak Valecha (@_palakvalecha_)
[Perfume Lover, Fragrance, Beauty, Trends 2026, Beauty Essentials, Summer, Gen Z]

posts.caption:
He made a dramatic debut last evening on one of the world’s most prestigious fashion stages—Paris Haute Couture Week 2026 (@hautecoutureweek)—and the accolades are still pouring in for Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05). The designer paid a deeply personal ode to his late mother with ‘Maa’, a collection rooted in memory and the unbreakable bond between mother and child.
Unfolding across four chapters, Cocoon, Bond, Becoming and Abundance, the collection moved from sculptural, protective silhouettes to fluid draped forms, from intricate vintage salli, zardozi and hand embroidery to monumental, richly embellished gowns.
Blush and rose tones inspired by his mother, her favourite florals, and jewellery-like embellishments glided through every look, taking memory into a beautiful display of couture. The show also saw Manish Malhotra High Jewellery’s (@manishmalhotrajewellery) new collection unveiled.
Also talked-about just as much were the evening’s starry front row guests—fashion icon Anna Wintour as well as corporate head honcho Isha Ambani and Chinese actress Fan Bingbing, while Karan Johar was also present at the event.
@hautecoutureweek
#ManishMalhotra
#ManishMalhotraWorld
#ManishMalhotraHighJewellery
#parishautecoutureweek

posts.caption:
When it comes to Indian weddings, there's an accessory that's part of every girl's ethnic ensemble. A lil pair of juttis that transitions from haldi afternoons to mehendi soirees, qawwali nights and main day rituals, too! And Fizzy Goblet's (@fizzygoblet) pretty juttis with their embellishments, threadwork and new silhouttes, have been dropping all the right swag. Founder and creative head Laksheeta Govil (@laksheeta) has whipped up a contemporary vibe for this footwear classic, pushing the envelope and loving every minute of it!
In her universe, neons go into a cool meltdown, metallics play elegant muse and florals pack all the right charm. Mules, heels, ballet flats and sneakers, added to the mix. And now, Bollywood star Kareena Kapoor Khan (@kareenakapoorkhan)-who's shared a long-time association with the brand-steps in as investor, deepening their partnership. We chat with Laksheeta on why treading new ground makes for so much fun.
Read the full story via the link in bio🔗
By Ismat Tahseen (@ismat.tahseen)
——
Manifest Approved: An interview with the people shaping the wedding industry, from designers to planners and cultural tastemakers. Each story explores the business, craft and evolving expectations behind modern weddings.

posts.caption:
Wedding invitations in 2026 are no longer just about announcing a date—they’re setting the tone for the entire celebration. Couples are embracing a blend of minimal aesthetics, immersive wedding websites, meaningful storytelling, and personalised details to create invitations.
Clearly, it’s a shift from traditional, and overly-decorative designs. The new vibe? Sculpted embossing and nostalgic Indian motifs that feature the beloved genda phool to destination-inspired artworks!
While digital platforms are making the guest experience smoother through custom websites and seamless RSVPs, these nouveau print invitations seem to be grabbing their own spotlight.
Read more via the link in bio🔗
By Srishti Mukherjee (@sri.sshhhhh)
[Wedding Invitation Trends, Shift in Trends 2026, Design and Aesthetic, Quirky Invites]
posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.

For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”

Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.

Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Pierpaolo Piccioli’s debut couture show for Balenciaga was a gorgeous parade of dramatic designs. The collection was recognized by many as a return to form, and while it was "beautiful, no question," as our chief fashion critic Vanessa Friedman wrote in her review, it also felt familiar.
Piccioli has embraced the extraordinary color sense that became his signature in his former job at Valentino, as well as the grace of form. He has combined that with classic Balenciaga shapes (the sack, the trapeze, the balloon skirt) made in an entirely contemporary way. On the surface, the designer is doing everything right. But is it right for right now?
Read @vvfriedman's full review of @balenciaga's Paris couture show at the link in our bio. Photos by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
Duran Lantink, the next-gen designer at Jean Paul Gaultier and the first permanent creative director to follow the brand’s founder, is a master provocateur.
It was Lantink, after all, who broke the internet last spring when he opened and closed a show for his own line with a man wearing a female chest prosthetic and a woman wearing a man’s. In October, he featured a bodysuit silk-screened with a naked male body, detailed genitalia and all, in his first Gaultier ready-to-wear show. Little wonder his Gaultier couture was the most-anticipated debut this week.
Nowadays, as fashion shows become more about driving engagement and name recognition, the more extreme and ridiculous a look, the better. And Lantink isn’t the only designer who seemingly strives for virality. Armani Privé, Robert Wun, Viktor and Rolf and Schiaparelli all made their bids for the attention economy. But some gimmicks work better than others. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman's analysis of the couture shows in Paris. Photos by @simbarashecha, Jean Paul Gaultier, Luca Tombolini, Umberto Fratini

posts.caption:
Over three weeks and across four different cities, our photographer @simbarashecha went to World Cup matches featuring teams from five continents, capturing the best soccer style.
Though the sport has yet to move fully into the American mainstream, the tournament’s return to U.S. soil has brought along with it breathtaking levels of enthusiasm in team identity. With flags, fringe, face paint and at least one horned helmet, fans from every country brought unique style and flair to the party.
Swipe through to see the World Cup fans who left it all on the pitch, and tap the link in our bio to see more photos.

posts.caption:
A reader asked our fashion critic: "I understand that we’re all dressing more casually these days, especially so since the pandemic. But there is something special about going to a play or a nice restaurant, and increasingly I’m seeing folks at such places dressed in what they’d wear to the supermarket or to do chores around the house — which kind of makes me sad. Are we losing something?"
The rising casualness of the world is making us reconsider what getting “dressed up” means. Is that a bad thing? At the link in our bio, read @vvfriedman's answer. Photo by Jeremy Moeller/Getty Images

posts.caption:
At Chanel's couture show on Tuesday in Paris, the designer Matthieu Blazy went beyond the super sucked-in, laced-up styles of the moment.
Since his debut for the brand last October, Blazy has been offering surprising, almost subversive collections designed to liberate the body beneath, rather than constrict. Amid the the recent dominance of the GLP-1 body, style has evolved to meet the demand for tinier clothing — waists snatched, skirts hobble-tight — with mermaid silhouettes and corset dresses ruling red carpets and runways.
Instead, Chanel offered storytelling, and space to move. Fairy tales were the inspiration for his collection; tales of childhood woven into the clothes on the runway. The airy shift dresses, mousseline skirts with pockets and even the tailored tweed looks were clearly made for movement. At the link in our bio, see more photos from @chanelofficial's couture collection and read @vvfriedman's full review. Photos by @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
At the Dior couture show on Monday in Paris, there was an elephant the room: the still-secret design of the Dior wedding dress Taylor Swift had worn a mere three days earlier.
Was it more classic — i.e., cliché — Dior, or the more current, somewhat challenging Dior? Was it princess-y? Prom-like? Kinda conceptual? Was it, in other words, more like the image that Swift has been presenting to the world, at least onstage, or more like the one that the newish designer Jonathan Anderson has been building for the brand?
The show didn’t necessarily offer any clues. But it did demonstrate why Swift might have thought Dior was the right brand for her in the first place. Anderson's work is "increasingly both notably intricate and apparently relaxed, a tough balancing act to pull off," wrote Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. At the link in our bio, see more photos from @dior's couture show and read @vvfriedman's full review. Photos by Emma Da Silva/@apnews, @simbarashecha

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha

posts.caption:
A college girl from Kolkata decided to take her first steps in the entertainment industry, albeit being besieged by loads of ‘what ifs’. What if she doesn’t get projects? What if her introverted behaviour prevents her from acting? Putting all these questions aside, she nursed her passion for being an actor.
That’s Tillotama Shome, an actor recognised for winning the trust of people, a storyteller who doesn’t hesitate in refusing scripts she doesn’t align with.
Born to parents who taught her the understanding of affection and freedom, she always aimed to be financially capable. She began her career with the film ‘Monsoon Wedding’ and built a career defined by versatility rather than convention.
Her feature film Boksho Bondi, also produced by her, was recently awarded by FIPRESCI (The International Federation of Film Critics).
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full interview by @_shivanilamba
#tillotamashome #bollywood #cinema Boksho Bondi, actress, body objectification, storytelling, film characters, entertainment industry, trending viral, Monsoon Wedding, Qissa, Acting

posts.caption:
The perfect way to unwind after a long day? Light a candle. Make some tea. Curl up with a good book, let the words wash over you, drift into something quiet and nourishing.
No. Open Instagram. Scroll until the candle burns out. Somewhere along the way, this became the unwind.
The most convenient explanation for this is that we’ve all rotted. The Instagram algorithm has hijacked our dopamine. Our attention spans have shrunk to the length of a YouTube Short. And us? Passive recipients of whatever the feed decides we deserve.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read more.
#brainrot Social media, brainrot content, Ananya Pandey, doom scroll, Instagram, trending, viral, explore, books, reading, trends, attention span

posts.caption:
When a woman decides to chase her dreams in India, loads of hurdles await her on her way. Be it society’s conservatism, stereotypes, or even families who preserve old misogynistic traditions. Hina Bano did not let her religion win over her ambitions, and she is now part of the Indian senior women's hockey team.
At the age of two, Hina lost her father. She was raised by her maternal grandfather, who was completely against women playing sports. Hina suppressed her passion for hockey until she met her coach, who encouraged her to play sports.
In a conversation with Shethepeople, Hina Bano talks about choosing sports against her family’s wish and shares how she keeps herself motivated on tough days.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full interview by @_shivanilamba
#hockey #sports Hina Bano, Indian hockey team, Indian sports, women in sports, trending, viral, explore, ambitions, conservativism, patriarchy, feminism, societal norms, stereotypes

This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.

PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.

Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com 

PRINT ISN’T DEAD.

🫂 @lilbieber 
📸 @sweetcian 
🔗 @freenjoy

posts.caption:
For decades, #Chanel couture shows have ended with a bridal look, but this time #MatthieuBlazy reimagined the traditional narrative as a story of independence. Matthieu was inspired by a book of fairy tales and #GabrielleChanel’s own story, from hardship to success. A wedding dress wasn’t Chanel’s fairytale because she never got married, so instead he closed with the “revenge dress.”
A tribute to Chanel’s life, which was defined by ambition, freedom, and creativity rather than marriage, while famously evoking the spirit of #PrincessDiana, a symbol of confidence and reclaiming one’s own narrative.
posts.alt_text: Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
#Givenchy Spring 1997 was #AlexanderMcqueens first collection after taking over from #JohnGalliano. It actually received mixed reactions, many felt McQueen was too young and too rebellious for a house built on traditional French elegance.
His appointment represented a huge shift in fashion, as younger designers began taking control of historic couture houses. McQueen himself later criticized the collection, but over time it became one of the most important moments in his career.
#Zendaya in @givenchy Spring 1997 and accompanying @philiptreacy headpiece, styled by @luxurylaw
posts.alt_text: Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture
Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
For his Fall 2026 #Schiaparelli Couture collection, #DanielRoseberry explained that he had deliberately travelled to #Barcelona in the hope that the city would inspire his new collection.
One of the collection’s key influences was the work of #AntoniGaudí and his distinctive architecture. Gaudí’s influence can be seen throughout several looks, particularly those inspired by the façade of #CasaBatlló and the sun mosaic at Park Güell.
Schiaprelli used intricate embroidery and embellishment, including real flowers that were fed sugar water to preserve their colour and flexibility before being carefully embroidered onto the garments. Echoing Gaudí’s iconic mosaic techniques and organic forms, transforming architectural details into wearable art.
posts.alt_text: Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
Ahead of #JonathanAnderson’s second couture debut, step inside the salons of 30 Avenue Montaigne and rediscover the work of #ChristianDior in the 1950s, a period that defined an era of fashion and cemented #Paris as the capital of #HauteCouture.
#Dior became renowned for his ability to reinvent the female silhouette almost every season. Throughout the 1950s, he continued to develop the New Look with its tiny, corseted waist, rounded shoulders, and full calf-length skirts crafted from luxurious silks, satins, taffetas, and organza.
posts.alt_text: Come take a closer look at #DanielRoseberry’s #Schiaparelli 2026 couture collection “The Abyss” inspired by the mysterious world beneath the ocean’s surface. #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
Founded in 1838, @charvet_official was one of the world’s first dedicated shirtmaker. It pioneered the concept of luxury bespoke shirting and has long been recognised as one of the most discreet and respected maisons.
A full circle moment, the shirts hold a special place in #GabrielleChanel’s own history. Her great love and inspiration, Arthur “Boy” Capel, was known to wear #Charvet shirts, and #Chanel famously drew on masculine tailoring to redefine women’s fashion.
For his debut @chanelofficial collection, #MatthieuBlazy continued his signature of elevating everyday essentials through exceptional craftsmanship and collaborated with Charvet on shirts, each finished with pearl buttons and Chanel’s signature chain sewn into the hem.

posts.caption:
Everyone spends months on the sherwani and five minutes on the drape — but the stole is what photographs in every wide shot, every entry video, every mandap close-up.
The single-shoulder drape: one end pinned, the rest falling loose down the back — built entirely for the entry walk.
The draped-and-tucked stole: across both shoulders then tucked into the waistband on one side, structured and formal, ideal for the mandap.
The contrast stole: a colour that fights the sherwani rather than matching it — the detail that stops a groom from disappearing into the background of his own wedding.
The layered dupatta-over-jacket: draped over an open bandhgala instead of a plain sherwani, adds volume without a heavier fabric underneath.
The Rajasthani-style safa dupatta pairing: stole in the same fabric or border as the turban, tying the whole look together from head to torso instead of treating them as two separate decisions.
#JustSpreehIt

posts.caption:
Everyone defaults to the full matching set — necklace, earrings, maang tikka, all from the same jeweller, all bought together. But the looks that actually photograph differently are the ones built from unexpected pairings. Polki choker with a single long haar: two neckpieces at completely different lengths, the negative space between them doing all the work. Temple maang tikka with a diamond necklace: old-world headpiece, modern stones — the contrast is the point. Single statement earring with a bare neck: one sculptural piece so heavy it needs nothing else, the collarbone left completely clean. Pearl choker with a haathphool: the neck kept soft, the hand turned into the statement. Matte gold set with one polki accent: everything warm and understated except for one piece that catches the light differently. Five pairings, zero matching sets.
#JustSpreehIt

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.

John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.

If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

posts.caption:
Your job title says “Fashion Stylist” but the actual job is about 10 roles wrapped into one.
You’re the creative director. The producer. The logistics coordinator. The sample tracker. The problem solver. The client handler. The backup plan when everything falls apart.
You manage difficult clients who change the brief 24 hours before the shoot. You chase payments that should’ve landed weeks ago. You track samples across continents praying nothing gets stuck in customs. You keep the whole production moving when chaos hits.
And you make it all look effortless.
That’s the skill nobody talks about. Making the final result look polished and intentional when behind the scenes you were juggling 15 moving parts and solving disasters in real time.
The problem is you’re doing the work of five different job roles but getting paid for one. Because you don’t know how to price for all of it. You don’t have systems to invoice for planning time or logistics coordination or the mental load of holding everything together.
You know the job is bigger than styling. You just don’t have the frameworks to charge properly for what you actually do.
Inside The Studio, we teach you how to price for the real job. Not just the styling bit. All of it. Rate frameworks that cover every part of the work. Contract templates that protect your time. Systems that help you stop underselling yourself at every turn.
If you’ve been building your career alone and it’s not working anymore, this is where you stop pretending you have it figured out.
➡️➡️ Comment STUDIO to join or follow link in bio 🔗

posts.caption:
How do you price yourself? Be honest.
Most rate problems aren’t confidence problems. They’re information problems. When you don’t know what your market actually pays, you’re left to guess, and guessing under pressure almost always slides low. The quick yes comes back, your stomach drops, because a fast yes usually means you went too cheap. Again.
Here’s the freeing part: the stylists who charge well aren’t bolder than you, and they’re not luckier. They price from information, not hope. They know what their sector pays, where they sit in it, and what their market expects. That’s not a personality trait. It’s research, and anyone can do it.
Your rate moves with three things. Your sector: editorial, commercial, celebrity and e-commerce all pay differently. Your stage: two years in shouldn’t charge like ten. Your market: London isn’t Manchester. Once you can see those three, your rate stops being a guess and becomes a decision, and a decision is something you can say without apologising for it.
So the real question: do you actually know what stylists at your level, in your sector, in your market are charging? Or are you hoping you’re close?
If it’s the second, that’s not a flaw. No one ever handed you the numbers. But you can go and find them, and the day you do, quoting stops being the part you dread.
The full Rate Workshop, pricing by sector, stage and market, lives inside the Studio.
Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you in free for 7 days. 💚
Em x

posts.caption:
If your main source of income disappeared tomorrow, what would you have? Not in six months. Not gradually. Tomorrow.
Editorial budgets get cut without warning. Commercial clients freeze campaigns overnight. Celebrity talent changes stylists on a whim. Editors move on and take their favourites with them. Brands go quiet after years of consistent bookings.
This isn’t pessimism. This is the reality of freelance life in fashion.
The stylists who feel secure right now aren’t luckier than you. They’re not more talented. They built more than one way to earn. They style and they consult. They work on set and they create content. They have commercial clients and editorial relationships and maybe something else entirely that uses their skills in a different way.
That’s not a lack of focus. That’s intelligence.
One income stream in this industry is a vulnerability. It means one client, one editor, one brand can change their mind and your entire career wobbles. Multiple income streams means you’re protected. Not immune to change, but not destroyed by it either.
The time to build this is not when work dries up. It’s now. While you’re busy. While things feel stable. That’s when you create the safety net.
Your skills transfer more than you think. Styling becomes consulting. Set experience becomes creative direction. Fashion knowledge becomes content. You have options you’ve never explored.
The industry isn’t dying. It’s sorting. Sorting the stylists who adapt from those who wait for things to go back to how they were.
Which group are you in?
Swipe through. This might change how you think about your career.
Comment STUDIO to join The Studio of Stylist Elixir free for 7 days.
posts.alt_text:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.

It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.

And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.

Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.

Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.

That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.

Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
Undercharging rarely feels like undercharging. It feels like a quick yes, a full calendar, a client who never blinks. And still, somehow, never quite enough at the end of the month.
It is not your worth. It is that no one ever showed you the number, and you have no one to check it against.
That is the whole thing we fix in the Studio.
Comment STYLIST and I’ll send you a free week.
#fashionstylist #personalstylist #freelancestylist #stylistlife #stylistcommunity

posts.caption:
Zendaya is currently putting together one of the strongest press tours in recent memory. Working alongside longtime stylist Law Roach, her wardrobe for The Odyssey has embraced true method dressing, leaning into Grecian drapery, sculptural corsetry, and silhouettes that feel worthy of the epic itself.
From custom Jacquemus to archival Givenchy and Schiaparelli couture literally straight off the runway, every look has balanced cinematic storytelling with impeccable craftsmanship.
At this point, it’s hard to think of anyone executing modern method dressing better than Zendaya.
#Zendaya #LawRoach #Schiaparelli #Givenchy #Jacquemus
posts.alt_text:
A little over a year into Jonathan Anderson’s Dior, and the verdict is… split. On one hand, we’re getting some collections that feel studied, referential, and intent on reshaping what Dior means now. On the other, a growing sense that something is missing: the fantasy, the excess, the unmistakable “Dior Myth” that once made the house feel untouchable.

The question is where does Dior go from here—deeper into fantasy, or back toward tradition? Drop your thoughts.

#Dior #JonathanAnderson

posts.caption:
I’ve been so busy daydreaming about this collection that I almost forgot to post it. Matthieu Blazy’s FW26 Haute Couture collection for Chanel transformed the Grand Palais into an enchanted storybook world for Gaby and the Beanstalk.
Inspired by a volume of fairy tales from Coco Chanel’s personal library, Blazy reimagined the house’s heritage through whimsical craftsmanship rather than nostalgia. The collection evolved from effortless daywear into ethereal evening looks, featuring sheer mousseline takes on the classic tweed suit, trompe l’œil textures, chain mail, and fluid pastel gowns.
The magic was in the details: buttons transformed from ugly ducklings into swans, heels became golden eggs, and miniature garden scenes were sculpted into the shoes themselves.
One thing about Matthieu Blazy—he knows how to tell a story.
#Chanel #MatthieuBlazy #HauteCouture

posts.caption:
Everyone say it with me: THANK YOU, PIERPAOLO!!!!! Pierpaolo Piccioli has officially begun a new era at Balenciaga, unveiling his highly anticipated haute couture debut, and I’m completely obsessed.
Stepping away from the conceptual irony that defined the previous era, Piccioli delivered a collection rooted in romance, emotion, and extraordinary craftsmanship. Rich color, fluid movement, sculptural silhouettes, and impeccable couture techniques reminded everyone that this label isn’t all about latex and leather…
The colors. The textures. The movement. The venue. The casting. Balenciaga feels romantic again, and I am so here for it.
Drop your thoughts.
#Balenciaga #PierpaoloPiccioli

posts.caption:
In India, antibiotics are often treated like everyday pills: easy to buy, easy to share, easy to take when rest feels inconvenient.
Limited sick leave, rushed consultations, time poverty, and over-the-counter access have made self-medication dangerously normal.
The result is antimicrobial resistance, where medicines stop working against infections they once treated. When antibiotics fail, routine illnesses become harder to cure and modern medicine loses one of its most essential safeguards.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#health #antimicrobialresistance #India #medicalcrisis #pillculture
posts.alt_text:
The interesting thing about sachi zari is that it complicates the idea of permanence. A sari may last for generations. Only practice preserves a craft.

At Shanti Banaras (@shantibanaras), creative director Khushi Shah (@khushishahh9) approaches that practice as an exchange between weaver, material, and design. Her role is less to reinvent its vocabulary than to understand how it might continue: through collaboration with weavers, new expressions of established techniques and, soon, digital records that make each sari’s provenance visible.

It raises a larger question: how does a tradition move forward without losing the knowledge that gives it meaning? By recognising continuity as a creative act, renewed each time knowledge passes from one hand to another and the loom begins again.

#TheCraftSeries #TheEstablished #ShantiBanaras

posts.caption:
Delhi is not a market Lululemon (@lululemon) needs to explain itself to. It has long been understood, just not officially stocked.
The Canadian brand’s first India store will open at DLF Promenade later this year through its franchise partnership with Tata CLiQ (@tatacliqfashion), converting years of airport hauls and global wardrobe fluency into local retail.
The sharper play is distribution: the Delhi store arrives alongside launches on Tata CLiQ Luxury and Tata CLiQ Fashion, making the debut physical in the capital but national online. The store will carry Lululemon’s technical apparel and lifestyle pieces across yoga, Pilates, running, training, tennis, golf, and everyday movement.

posts.caption:
Everyone is chasing new AI job titles. In India, the real shift is happening inside old roles.
Software engineers, educators, marketers, and reviewers are finding that the job title may have survived, but the job description has not. AI is now stitched into daily workflows, changing expectations faster than titles can keep up.
Then there is the invisible workforce: annotators, moderators, and trainers who keep the system running, but rarely get counted in the AI jobs boom.
The story of AI jobs in India is really a story of work being rewritten in real time.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Saumya Rastogi (@saumyaarastogi)
#AIJobs #WorkCulture #IndiaWorkforce #AIImpact #FutureOfJobs

posts.caption:
Long before gelato became a personality type and desserts started arriving in clean-label tubs, cassata ice cream had already perfected the drama.
Inspired by the Italian cassata dessert, it became something entirely its own in India: sponge cake, tutti-frutti, pista, strawberry, and synthetic colour, all stacked into one slice that felt fancier than it had any right to.
For many Indians, cassata ice cream was childhood luxury. Wedding buffets, old-school parlours, family dinners, freezer boxes opened for guests. A dessert that looked like occasion, even before anyone knew how to describe it.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#FoodHistory #Cassata #IndianDesserts #Nostalgia #Culture

posts.caption:
Rahul Mishra’s (@rahulmishra_7) couture is easiest to understand through the designer he has always tried to be: a storyteller who sees fashion as an ecosystem of labour, memory, craft, and belief.
That impulse has shaped his work for years, from his commitment to artisan communities to the way he has steadily expanded the language of Indian craft. Those who have watched Mishra move from Indian fashion weeks to winning the International Woolmark Prize and now showing at the Paris Haute Couture Week have also watched a designer grow increasingly confident in his own vocabulary.
That confidence sits at the centre of his latest couture collection, DEVI: The Eternal Muse. Mishra is no longer simply translating Indian history for an international room. He is excavating it. The collection experiments with trompe-l’œil surfaces that suggest sandstone, basalt, and bronze rather than fabric. What holds it together is the way Mishra makes cloth carry the weight of stone. Zardozi, dabka, crystals, stones, and bugle beads build depth and texture, turning embroidery into architecture and couture into moving sculpture.
And yet, DEVI is where Mishra’s defining strength begins to reveal its limits. The handwork is extraordinary, but the idea sometimes becomes too literal, too heavily loaded. The goddess, the muse, the artefact, the monument: the woman is asked to hold so much symbolism that she risks becoming less human than icon.
What the collection reveals is not whether Indian couture can hold its own in Paris. That argument is already dated. Mishra’s craft is never in question. What it needs now is risk: a willingness to let beauty become less obedient, less reverent, and perhaps more uncomfortable.
📸: Chloé Rasquinet (@lookwhatyoumademesee), Arina Prikhodko (@arineeel), Gourab Ganguli (@gourabganguli), Moral moda (@moralmoda_visionnaire), karlaotto (@karlaotto) ,silxstaryst, Mateusz Pielesz (@mateuszpielesz)

posts.caption:
The old celebrity deal was a face on a campaign. The new one is a face on the cap table. For years, Indian celebrities have helped consumer brands borrow trust, speed up discovery, and make products feel culturally inevitable. Kareena Kapoor Khan’s (@kareenakapoorkhan ) investment in Fizzy Goblet (@fizzygoblet) is best read against the financial value and earning power of her public image. In 2024, Kapoor Khan’s brand value was estimated at $79.5 million by Kroll.
Kapoor Khan’s move from endorsement to equity asks why that value should end at a campaign fee. Her earlier bets, from Pluckk to Quench Botanics, follow the same logic, revealing an understanding of the consumer economy orbiting her: the food women want to eat healthier, the skincare they are curious about, the festive fashion they want to be seen in.
With Fizzy Goblet, that equation becomes cleaner. The amount remains undisclosed, but the value of the deal is not limited to the cheque. It converts a familiar celebrity association into a growth signal at a moment when the brand is chasing scale, retail expansion, and sharper consumer recall. Kapoor Khan has already helped make Fizzy Goblet more visible and more legible to a consumer who wants Indian craft without the heaviness of heritage retail.
The investment reflects a broader shift in how influence is being monetised in India. Celebrity image is now being treated as an asset class, with equity as the more ambitious end of the deal.

posts.caption:
sometimes the most exciting collections aren’t coming from the biggest fashion houses.
Central Saint Martins student Enzo Keuning approaches fashion almost like an engineer for his pre-collection. originally trained in Econometrics, his work combines mathematics, material innovation and traditional craftsmanship to create garments that feel somewhere between nature and technology.
his latest pre-collection, “Take Me, Nature, I’m Your Bride”, reimagines Dutch ceremonial dress through an entirely new lens. intricate pleats become hand-built interlocking leather textiles, garments are assembled without stitching, preserved botanicals sit alongside engineered materials, and silhouettes gradually peel away from historical dress codes into something sensual, fluid and almost futuristic.
it’s bridalwear, craftsmanship and material experimentation all at once, as well as proof that some of the most interesting ideas in fashion are still coming from the next generation.
#TFO #CentralSaintMartins #designer #fashiondesigner #fashion

Viktor & Rolf will always make sure to make a statement, and it really never is a miss

posts.caption:
Even if you know nothing about Masaba Gupta (@masabagupta), at the very least you know that she’s famous. Some people know her simply as Masaba, founder of the fashion label House of Masaba (@houseofmasaba) that, for the last 17 years, has been making playfully printed contemporary Indian clothing. Others know her as the star of the Netflix show ‘Masaba Masaba’. To the chronically online, she’s the powerful influencer, the person brands go to when they want a cool, articulate personality to represent them. The makeup-obsessed know her via LoveChild by Masaba (@lovechildbymasaba), the beauty brand she started in 2021, which has taken her obsession with makeup and turned it into products built specifically around Indian skin tones.
Yet, as she continues to do it all, there is a change underway. As we walk out of her office, Masaba casually drops a piece of news. “I’m moving away from ready-to-wear entirely. I think spring/summer 2026 is the last one we’ll do. The focus is going to be on occasion and bridalwear.” It’s a pretty big declaration, and a timely one, considering that later this month Masaba will be a part of the FDCI Hyundai India Couture Week in New Delhi. Right now, it feels like Masaba is moving into a new era, one where she’s more of a founder and businesswoman than a niche fashion designer.
At the link in bio, @butoolbegum spends the day in the company of the enterprising designer, business owner, and new mom.

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Music has quietly become one of our biggest personality traits. It soundtracks our morning commutes, gets us through workouts, plays in the background of every late-night drive, and somehow always knows exactly what mood we’re in. So honestly, it was only a matter of time before it made its way into our fragrance wardrobe too. That’s exactly what @fastrackperfumes has done with DIVINE.
Inspired by three of the rapper’s most iconic tracks, the new fragrance collection translates the energy, attitude, and mood of each song into scent. Safe to say, this might just be one of the coolest ways we’ve seen music step off the playlist and into everyday life.
#Ad

posts.caption:
Everyone already knows Prateek Kuhad (@prateekkuhad), has gone well beyond being a reluctant indie music maverick into his “main character era”. On his third full-length album, 'Full Moon Chamber'— a fully English project made in the US and released today—he’s seen driving a car through the desert with love on his mind (‘If I Cannot Be Yours’), captured in a deep, serene embrace on his album cover art and using words like “resy”.
He’s also still one to take his own time. The collaborators may have changed—this time, Kuhad enlisted LA-based producers Nick Ruth (@ripruthtruth) and Mike Irish (@_mikeirish)—but his meticulousness has not. Just when the album felt like it was ready in late 2024, a few delays from his label Atlantic Records led him to relook at the project. “I was kind of listening back to that version of the record and I was like, it needs more cohesion, it needs a little bit more. I realised I was not very happy with the vocal takes and stuff,” he admits.
In 2025, he connected with Nick Ruth (whose production credits include pop star turned talk show host Kelly Clarkson) and they were in agreement that the album “just wasn’t hitting” the way he wanted. “We just really worked for a couple of months on really polishing it. I did all the vocal takes again, we did a bunch of new guitars, we did drums—mostly all new. Aaron Sterling [whose past session work includes songs by John Mayer, Taylor Swift, Harry Styles and more], an absolute G, played on it. And then it sounded absolutely incredible,” Kuhad adds excitedly.
At the link in bio, the LA-based singer-songwriter talks about the making of 'Full Moon Chamber', what it’s really like living between two continents, and why Erewhon is overrated.
Photography: @saranggupta

posts.caption:
After keeping us waiting for 'dul tagrep' (two years in Minionese), Gru’s little fellows are back with a new adventure. And this time, you can expect more than just a good laugh.
In ‘Minions & Monsters’ (@minions), the Minions are in search of a new master to serve. After a series of mishaps (which involve toilet paper and a spell book), they find themselves in 1920s Hollywood. They soon become the toast of town, until the arrival of the talkies renders them obsolete—’coz, of course, no one understands Minionese. They then decide to make a movie, and, in the process, do the most Hollywood thing: revisit nuggets of Hollywood history, from classic slapstick references like Charlie Chaplin to early film history, when colour and sound were off the table.
At the link in bio, @nandini_sanghvi and @ara_mishra with the Hollywood references that filmmaker Pierre Coffin dropped like Easter eggs in the film for nerds to totally geek out on.

posts.caption:
𝐏𝐚𝐧𝐞𝐥 𝐈𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬 | 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐬 𝐒𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐀𝐜𝐫𝐨𝐬𝐬 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐂𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬
𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲? 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫 𝐢𝐬 𝐟𝐚𝐫 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐧𝐮𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐩𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐨𝐫 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫.
At 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐍𝐞𝐰 𝐒𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲, 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 & 𝐂𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞, organised by the @indofrenchchamber in collaboration with the @comitecolbert in Paris, an engaging panel explored how India's luxury landscape is being reshaped by identity, craft, culture and evolving consumer aspirations.
The session was moderated by @shefatwork, Editor-in-Chief, The Voice of Fashion and Atlas of Affluence, and featured an esteemed panel comprising:
* @karishmaswali77, Creative Director, Chanakya International and Chanakya School of Craft; Founder and Chair, Chanakya Foundation; Co-Founder and Creative Director, Chorus
* Vikram Goyal, Founder, @vikramgoyalstudio
* @rvaziralli, General Manager, The @esteelaudercompanies India, (@esteelauderin)
The conversation highlighted several defining shifts in India's luxury ecosystem:
✨ 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨 𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐥𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞𝐫.
✨ 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚 𝐩𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐟𝐮𝐥 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲.
✨ 𝐇𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐠𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐧𝐨𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐠𝐢𝐚—𝐢𝐭'𝐬 𝐚 𝐟𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧.
✨ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐧𝐞𝐱𝐭 𝐰𝐚𝐯𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐛𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐆𝐞𝐧 𝐙.
✨ 𝐋𝐨𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐯𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐝𝐫𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐫 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬.
#IFCCI #Luxury #LuxuryIndia #LuxuryConsumer #IndiaLuxury #Craftsmanship #Design #Innovation #IndiaFrance #Paris
@payalskanwarofficial | @sripeepul | @swagata.btr | @anuragty | @shwets.pahuja | @rishimoony13 | @iccrinfrance | @indiainfrance | @franceinindia
posts.alt_text:
✨ 𝐅𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐞 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐭: 𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐈𝐧𝐟𝐥𝐮𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐀𝐬𝐩𝐢𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 – 𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐃𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐕𝐨𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐬 𝐀𝐫𝐞 𝐒𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲 𝐃𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐝

As part of 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐍𝐞𝐰 𝐒𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲, 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 & 𝐂𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 organised by the @indofrenchchamber Luxury Committee and @comitecolbert in Paris, this engaging fireside chat explored the evolving dynamics of India's luxury consumer.

Moderated by @sripeepul *, Chairperson, IFCCI Luxury Committee & Founder, Peepul Advisory, the conversation featured @masoomminawala, Entrepreneur, Content Creator & Influencer.

The discussion highlighted that meaningful engagement with the Indian luxury market goes far beyond market entry. It requires a deep understanding of India's cultural nuances, evolving consumer aspirations and rich craft heritage. As global brands look toward the future, investing in India's creative ecosystem will be key to shaping the next chapter of luxury.

𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐲 𝐭𝐮𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐚𝐬 𝐰𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐮𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐬𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐡𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐍𝐞𝐰 𝐒𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐩𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐋𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲, 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 & 𝐂𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞, 𝐜𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐛𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫 𝐨𝐟 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐨-𝐅𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐡 𝐜𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐠𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐥𝐮𝐱𝐮𝐫𝐲.

#IFCCI #LuxuryCommittee #ComiteColbert #Luxury #DigitalInfluence #LuxuryMarketing #IndiaFrance #Craftsmanship #Culture #Innovation #Leadership

@payalskanwarofficial | @sripeepul | @swagata.btr | @anuragty | @shwets.pahuja | @rishimoony13 | @iccrinfrance | @indiainfrance | @franceinindia

posts.caption:
“It would be unfair, even inaccurate, to deny the scale of work and craftsmanship on display. Malhotra, the irrepressible Bollywood-stylist-turned-couturier, has always understood the emotional charge of clothes, especially those designed for the grand entrance, the close-up, the wedding album and the cinematic reveal. But what was hard to ignore was how closely his work leaned into every cliché in Indian fashion’s new global couture playbook. The cocoon structure, overblown volume, bustiers and breastplates, exaggerated shoulders, embroidered armour, and a language overawed by technique.” More on Manish Malhotra’s much-anticipated Paris Haute Couture Week debut at link in bio.
🖋️ @kimidangor
@manishmalhotraworld
#thevofashion #manishmalhotra #parisfashion

posts.caption:
At 𝐒𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐚𝐦, cloth stood taller than couture. Presented alongside Paris Haute Couture Week, Vaishali S’s collection reaffirmed the versatility of Indian handloom and the designer’s ability with textile engineering. Yet the demands of a global couture collection need much more than textile eloquence. Swayam fell short on that count. Read Shefalee Vasudev’s complete review at link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork
@vaishalisstudio
#thevofashion #textiles

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
It’s amazing how this image of a spider keeps returning throughout weaving myths and across countries that would have had no contact with each other.
In Navajo and Hopi tradition it’s described through two seperate beings, Mama Ocllo and Spider Grandmother.
*Typo on slide 5, it should say ‘This ISN’T ancient folklore…’ sorry 😅
Swipe to learn more and listen to the full episode on The Culture of Cloth wherever you get your podcasts 🎧
Cover art is by Susan Seddon Boulet

posts.caption:
This was a huge milestone for me because it taught me something I needed to learn.
Working in the fashion industry taught me that what I physically produced and created was equivalent to my worth.
Images and visuals are the primary language in the fashion industry, especially when communicating with manufacturing teams whose first language might not be english.
So I didn’t put much value on my writing or my philosophical views around fashion and culture. It was always a dialogue that was happening inside me, something I didn’t think connected to my sewing work.
But this year I’ve really leant into writing and finding my voice on Substack.
I think I can get hung up on thinking I need to sound a certain way to be taken seriously, so it’s been a learning curve to find what my voice actually sounds like.
I’m still learning and figuring it out but I’m really enjoying the dynamic of Substack and the space to write out my thoughts when they don’t fit into an Instagram carousel.
So here are my articles since I started in March.
Read the full articles via the link in my bio 🔗
First slide image is my Cordelia sewing pattern with my Hera sleeve sewing pattern mixed together 🥰 shop via the link in my bio

posts.caption:
Rembrandt once gave us instructions on how to view his work….”Don’t stand too close.” he said.
This week was session 3 of @readingsbooks Big Book Club on The Odyssey and we learned a little history about oral poetry.
Dr Tom Hercules Davies, a comparative philologist at @unimelb actually sung a few lines from the poem to help us understand the important of the musicality of the text.
Genuinely, it was so fascinating!
Read the full essay via the link on my bio
In partnership with @readingsbooks

You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.

The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.

The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.

Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.

Stockists
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .

Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .

Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.

For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
For our June cover story, we headed to one of our favorite Adriatic islands, Hvar, with one of fashion’s most captivating couples, @felicenova and @yslil. Surrounded by early summer sun, crystal clear water, and quiet Mediterranean landscapes, Felice stepped in front of Khalil’s camera for the first time, turning their relationship into a story of its own.
Photo: @yslil
Model: @felicenova @thelionsmgmt wearing @gucci
Fashion: @tangino
Editor-in-Chief: @milandjacic
Makeup: @sasajokovic
Hair: Milena Maršić @salonglamourzagreb
Creative Production: @ritama.b
Photo Assistant: @costanza_canali
Vogue Adria, June 2026
posts.alt_text:
“Until now, I somehow felt most at home in sadness, pain and falling apart. Now I feel that same sense of ease when I’m optimistic and anchored in brighter emotions. Today, I’m aware of the beauty of life, and most importantly, I believe I deserve it and that I’m worthy of it.”
One of the most influential artists on the regional scene, @reljarelja is entering a new, cinematic chapter of his career with the Varljivo leto trilogy. Ahead of the release of his new music, we spoke with him about the inspiration behind the project, the films that shaped it, and how his own life experiences became woven into the story. Read more, link in bio.

Photo: @moshicreative 
Styling: @stefanorlic 
Makeup: @jovana.mua 

Art Direction : Dragana Krtinić (@smisa0)

posts.caption: A cherry detail here or a blueberry decal there on your summer mani is usually expected. But this year, everyone is leaning all the way into the fruit fantasia and going bigger—and dare we say juicier—than before. Looking for some fruity inspo? Tap the link in our bio for seven delicious nail designs to try out. Photo: @sansungnails
posts.alt_text:
Hair loss, be it seasonal shedding, postpartum hair loss, or androgenetic alopecia, is incredibly discouraging, which may explain why the market is packed with products that have big (but often empty) promises. But the sole FDA-approved over-the-counter ingredient for hair growth, minoxidil, stands out for its time-tested, science-backed ability to help stymie hair loss and support hair growth. At the link in bio, discover everything you need to know about this incomparable ingredient, directly from the experts. 
Photographed by Irving Penn, Vogue, April 1984

posts.caption:
In an industry where the typical sample is a size zero, weight has always been central to a model’s success. But now, GLP-1 weight-loss medications offer a tempting shortcut to thinness. Models, modeling agents, casting directors, and designers are all noting a startling return to skinniness on the runway. “Models have definitely gotten smaller,” says @_ZoeLatta_ of the New York fashion brand @Eckhaus_Latta. “I think it's a combination of GLP-1s and a pendulum swing to extreme thinness being an idealized look again.” At the link in our bio, @RorySat explores how semaglutides and more GLP-1s have affected the modeling industry as we know it.
Photo: Turkina Faso

posts.caption: Size inclusivity in menswear remains largely static across #MilanFashionWeek and #ParisFashionWeek, with this season also welcoming more polarized body ideals. Alongside the continued dominance of straight-size casting, there was a sharp focus on extreme physiques, from "protein chic" and hyper-muscular builds shaped by looksmaxxing, to the return of ultra-slim "slender man" silhouettes. We found that of the thousands of looks presented across Milan and Paris SS27 shows, only 0.2% were shown on plus-size models, remaining unchanged since FW26. Tap the link in bio for a break down of the full report.
posts.alt_text: The latex look is having a moment. On the runways, both latex and latex-looking materials have dominated this summer — ironic, given it’s on track to be one of the hottest. It’s making a mark in pop culture, too. At @YSL’s SS27 show in Paris, mid-heatwave, #ConnorStorrie rocked up in knee-high latex sock boots. Once relegated to subcultures, will this trend translate to ready-to-wear — and sales? @MaddieeSchulz speaks to those in the industry to get their thoughts.

posts.caption:
On Thursday evening, inside a white-brick warehouse in Paris’s Belleville neighborhood, Martin Margiela’s archive went under the gavel in a one-of-a-kind auction marking the first time a living designer has directly collaborated with an auction house to sell their personal collection.
Over five hours, ‘Martin Margiela: Personal Archives’ saw almost 200 lots go under the hammer, including select pieces from his Hermès years, sourced from the wardrobe of his late mother, Léa Boucher, and unique pieces he had created during the pandemic. Most bidders in attendance were fashion archivists, fans, or museums, with the auction raising over €1.3 million, a portion of which will go to AIDS charities.
At the link in bio, contributor @SLWilson goes inside the auction, speaking with organizers about how it came together and attendees about what they got their hands on.
Auction Photos: Niña Slavcheva

Personalized shopping app @Daydream — which is basically ChatGPT for when you're binging #SummerHouse (post-scandal) and want to find one of the matching sets #PaigeDeSorbo wore in a previous season — is celebrating its first birthday. @Lisa_Verde, co-founder and chief brands officer sat down with @ElektraKotsoni to discuss what's next for the app, the lessons learned from its first year in business, and her advice on making it in America.
Get this week's Scoop at the link in bio — and sign up to receive it in your inbox every Friday.

posts.caption:
This season proved how 'hot' couture can be — quite literally. As guests navigated a scorching #HauteCoutureWeek in Paris with fans in hand, the season continued to thrive, supported by high-net-worth clients whose appetite for exclusivity and one-of-a-kind craftsmanship remains strong. #FW26, which ran from July 6 to 9, saw two couture debuts — @Pppiccioli at @Balenciaga and @DuranLantinkYo at @JeanPaulGaultier — as well as three sophomore couture shows from @Jonathan.Anderson at @Dior, @Matthieu_Blazy at @ChanelOfficial, and Silvana Armani at @GiorgioArmani Privé. At the link in bio, @LaureGuilbault shares the key takeaways to note.
Photo: @styledumonde

posts.caption: The walk-on look has arrived as tennis’s answer to the NBA tunnel walk. @NaomiOsaka’s custom kimono-inspired gown by @_HanaYagi at Wimbledon last week marked a new era of pre-match style — one where fashion becomes part of the game. From Osaka’s custom @Nike kits by @RobertWun to @Taylor_Fritz’s bespoke white @Boss suit, players are turning the walk onto court into a moment of self-expression, storytelling, and spectacle. At the link in bio, @LadyMollyElizabeth explores how, as more players embrace the pre-match style moment, more brands are tapping in.

posts.caption:
For their first Vogue India cover together, Padmanabh Singh (@pachojaipur) and Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) turn a day in Singapore into a sibling sport. Born one year apart, they grew up like twins with very different operating systems. Gauravi is “the planner, the pseudo-mom”, while Padmanabh continues to baffle her with spontaneous travel plans and a living-out-of-a-suitcase lifestyle.
Read the July-August cover story at the link in bio.
Head of Editorial Content: Rochelle Pinto (@rochelle.pinto)
Photographer: Zantz Han (@zantz)
Stylist: Manglien Gangte (@manglien)
Words by: Lamiya Chitalwalla (@lamiyachitalwalla)
Hair: Zhou Aiyi (@zhouaiyimua)
Makeup: Victoria Hwang (@vichwangmua)
Bookings Editor: Aliza Fatma (@alizaafatmaa)
Entertainment Director: Megha Mehta (@magzmehta)
Producer: Emily Teng (@eemileet)
Location Courtesy: Singapore Tourism Board (@visit_singapore)
Hospitality Partner: Frasers House, a Luxury Collection Hotel, Singapore (@frasershousesingapore)
On Gauravi: Dress, MAISON MARGIELA (@maisonmargiela). Clash de Cartier earrings, Long Clash de Cartier necklace, Juste un Clou bracelet, Juste un Clou classic bracelet, Juste un Clou ring, all CARTIER (@cartier).
On Padmanabh: Jacket, trousers; both HERMÈS (@hermes). Panthère de Cartier watch, Clash de Cartier bracelet; both CARTIER (@cartier).

posts.caption:
If you ask actor Akansha Ranjan Kapoor (@akansharanjan) how she met her now-husband, filmmaker Sharan Sharma (@sharanssharma), she’ll light up with glee. The two were introduced through a mutual friend who decided to play matchmaker. The couple held an intimate ceremony in the garden of their apartment building. It featured handwritten vows, a signing ceremony, speeches by loved ones, a champagne toast and acoustic music. For all the conversations around stressful wedding planning, the process only strengthened their partnership. “People always say couples fight the most while planning a wedding. But I feel like we became a dream team. We were like co-founders of a startup. Now, I think we can handle just about anything,” says Akansha Ranjan Kapoor.
Words by Thea Mulchandani (@thea.mulchandani)
Photographed by Naman Verma (@namanverma)
#VogueWeddings

Shree Jee Jewellers brings together the many facets of Jaipur’s jewellery tradition: from rare coloured gemstones and intricate lapidary work to rose cuts and fine diamonds. Every piece is shaped entirely in-house, allowing craftsmanship and material to evolve as one.
Discover their collection at Vogue Wedding Atelier 2026, curated as India’s most luxurious showcase of bridal couture and high jewellery. Request an invite at the link in bio.
@shreejeejaipur

posts.alt_text:
For their first Vogue India cover together, Padmanabh Singh (@pachojaipur) and Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) turn a day in Singapore into a sibling sport. Born one year apart, they grew up like twins with very different operating systems. Gauravi is “the planner, the pseudo-mom”, while Padmanabh continues to baffle her with spontaneous travel plans and a living-out-of-a-suitcase lifestyle.

Read the July-August cover story at the link in bio.
